Welcome to the show. Today we're talking about how small engineering teams at
Series A startups ship production ML features faster than much larger teams at
big tech companies. My guest is a staff engineer who has been on both sides.

The first observation is that infrastructure scope is inversely correlated with
shipping speed. When you have three Kubernetes clusters, seven service meshes
and a central platform team, every feature becomes a ticket in someone else's
queue. At a ten-person startup, the engineer who wants to ship a model owns
the whole stack — inference server, deployment, monitoring. There's no one to
wait for.

The second observation is about evaluation. Big tech treats eval as a process:
quarterly benchmarks, offline datasets, approval gates. Startups treat eval as
a scratchpad: how does this feel, is it better than what we had, ship it and
watch the metrics. Neither is right. The startup approach catches bugs the
benchmark misses. The big-tech approach catches regressions the scratchpad
misses. The interesting engineers do both — lightweight online eval plus a
handful of hard offline tests they actually trust.

The third observation is about cost. When an LLM feature costs five cents per
call and you have a hundred users, you don't care. When it costs five cents
per call and you have ten million users, it's a business. The engineering
culture that treats cost as a first-class constraint from day one wins the
second half of the product lifecycle. Most teams discover cost too late.

The last thing I want to talk about is model routing. A lot of teams default
to the biggest model for every query. That's lazy and expensive. Route easy
queries through small models, hard queries through big ones. A well-tuned
router cuts inference cost by sixty to seventy percent without changing
end-user quality. This is the highest-leverage optimization most gen AI
products are not doing yet.

If you take one thing from this episode: treat evaluation, cost, and routing
as first-class engineering concerns, not afterthoughts. That's the gap
between a demo and a product.
